content: A topos with NNO satisfies the continuum hypothesis iff for any object XXX and monomorphisms N↪X↪PN\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow X \hookrightarrow \mathcal{P}\mathbb{N}N↪X↪PN, one of them has to be an isomorphism. Does this imply anything about the logic of the topos? For example, is a CH topos always Boolean?
I was well into a long answer to this by the time I realised that statement of CH is wrong :relieved:
To be fair to you, this is also wrong on the nlab!!! (I'll change it tomorrow if someone hasn't already)

Suppose we work in a category Set\mathbf{Set}Set of ZFC sets satisfying the continuum hypothesis (because Gödel says we can). The canonical map N↪2N\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow 2^{\mathbb{N}}N↪2N sends each element to the classifying map for the singleton. But even ignoring that, consider ANY injective function N↪2N\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow 2^{\mathbb{N}}N↪2N, and let x∈2Nx \in 2^{\mathbb{N}}x∈2N be any element not in the image. Then we have inclusions N↪N∪{x}↪2N\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N} \cup \{x\} \hookrightarrow 2^{\mathbb{N}}N↪N∪{x}↪2N, and neither is an isomorphism... Oops!

Yup you're right. Ok so a topos satisfies CH iff there does not exist an object XXX with monomorphisms N↪X↪PN\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow X \hookrightarrow \mathcal{P}\mathbb{N}N↪X↪PN such that XXX is not isomorphic to either.

Anyway, I think the answer is no, it's not necessarily Boolean. I'm no expert, but I think the idea is that already CH fails on presheaves on the Cohen poset, because the Cohen poset is tailored to make that happen, and this presheaf topos is not Boolean. Most of the rest of the development from there is technical (pass to double negation sheaves to enforce Booleanness, pass to a filterquotient to enforce 2-valuedness).

I can't think of specific propositional properties of Ω\OmegaΩ that would be entailed by violation of CH. I should think that such violation would be "the norm".

For a hyperconnected Grothendieck topos, we get the following situation:

Let f:E→Setsf : \mathcal{E} \to \mathbf{Sets}f:E→Sets be the unique geometric morphism. Since this is hyperconnected, we have that the inverse image functor f∗f^*f∗ is fully faithful, and the image of f∗f^*f∗ is closed under subobjects. The sheaves in the image of f∗f^*f∗ are the "discrete sheaves" and the full subcategory of discrete sheaves is equivalent to the category of sets. The natural number object is f∗Nf^*\mathbb{N}f∗N, so this is a discrete sheaf as well. On the other hand, PN\mathcal{P}\mathbb{N}PN does not have to be discrete. But I think the inclusion f∗(2)⊆Ωf^*(2) \subseteq \Omegaf∗(2)⊆Ω will induce an inclusion f∗(2N)⊆PNf^*(2^\mathbb{N}) \subseteq \mathcal{P}\mathbb{N}f∗(2N)⊆PN.

Now there are two cases:

  • The sheaf PN\mathcal{P}\mathbb{N}PN is not discrete. In this case, f∗(2N)f^*(2^\mathbb{N})f∗(2N) is a counterexample to CH: it is not isomorphic to f∗(N)f^*(\mathbb{N})f∗(N) and because it is discrete it is also not isomorphic to PN\mathcal{P}\mathbb{N}PN.
  • The sheaf PN\mathcal{P}\mathbb{N}PN is discrete. Then because subsheaves of discrete sheaves are again discrete, we get that any counterexample to CH must be of the form f∗(X)f^*(X)f∗(X) for some set XXX. So in this case, CH holds in E\mathcal{E}E if and only if CH holds in Sets\mathbf{Sets}Sets.

So I agree with @Fawzi Hreiki and @Todd Trimble that failure of CH seems to be the norm.

For hyperconnected Grothendieck toposes, we have the necessary condition that if CH holds, then PN\mathcal{P}\mathbb{N}PN is discrete. I think PN=∏n∈NΩ\mathcal{P}\mathbb{N} = \prod_{n \in \mathbb{N}} \OmegaPN=∏n∈N​Ω, so if this is discrete then also the subobject classifier Ω\OmegaΩ must be discrete, since we have the diagonal embedding Ω→∏n∈NΩ\Omega \to \prod_{n \in \mathbb{N}} \OmegaΩ→∏n∈N​Ω. But if Ω\OmegaΩ is discrete, then necessarily Ω=f∗(2)\Omega = f^*(2)Ω=f∗(2) (by checking the universal property against other discrete sheaves), in other words E\mathcal{E}E is boolean.

So this shows that in this case CH implies booleanness, as you suspected. Unfortunately, this proof only works for hyperconnected Grothendieck toposes, maybe it can be extended to other cases as well.

Here's a sketch of how I think one could complete Jens' analysis: let E\mathcal{E}E be a topos over a topos of ZFC sets, Set\mathbf{Set}Set, and consider a subterminal object UUU distinct from 000. I claim that if N+U≅N\mathbb{N}+U \cong \mathbb{N}N+U≅N, then U≅1U \cong 1U≅1. This relies on countable extensivity, to the effect that the coproduct decomposition of N\mathbb{N}N pulls back along the isomorphism, and one of the pieces must be (part of) UUU, and hence this must be isomorphic to 111.
If that all works out, then since we always have N↪N+U↪N+1≅N\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N}+U \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N} + 1 \cong \mathbb{N}N↪N+U↪N+1≅N, we conclude that CH implies two-valuedness, and hence that E\mathcal{E}E is hyperconnected over Set\mathbf{Set}Set, so we can apply Jens' argument to deduce Booleanness.

Personally, I find this dissatisfying, and would prefer a formulation of CH that produces some more interesting cases. For example, we could demand that whenever N↪X↪ΩN\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow X \hookrightarrow \Omega^{\mathbb{N}}N↪X↪ΩN, there exists either a monomorphism X↪NX \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N}X↪N or ΩN↪X\Omega^{\mathbb{N}} \hookrightarrow XΩN↪X. This recovers the usual notion of cardinality in terms of injections in Set\mathbf{Set}Set, but avoids the cheap trick for localic toposes I describe above.

Incidentally, I just checked in Sheaves in Geometry and Logic, and the version that Mac Lane and Moerdijk go for is to show that in the Cohen topos there is an object KKK with monomorphisms N↪K↪ΩN\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow K \hookrightarrow \Omega^{\mathbb{N}}N↪K↪ΩN but no epimorphisms N↠K\mathbb{N} \twoheadrightarrow KN↠K or K↠ΩNK \twoheadrightarrow \Omega^{\mathbb{N}}K↠ΩN. Assuming choice (to make all the sets decidable), for non-empty sets this is equivalent to saying there are no monomorphisms in the other direction, but if we're considering CH without choice, I prefer a formulation which makes things more interesting!

Interesting that the stronger formulation of CH even forces a Grothendieck topos to be both two-valued/hyperconnected and boolean!

There is probably no single formulation of CH that is seen as the "standard" in topos theory. Another formulation closer to the original one would be to ask whether N⊆X⊆R\mathbb{N} \subseteq X \subseteq \mathbb{R}N⊆X⊆R implies X≅NX \cong \mathbb{N}X≅N or X≅RX \cong \mathbb{R}X≅R, where N\mathbb{N}N is a natural number object and R\mathbb{R}R is e.g. the Dedekind real numbers.
Right, but again that falls into the same issue that I pointed out involving subterminals. I still think it should be presented with monomorphisms rather than isomorphisms

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

Here's a sketch of how I think one could complete Jens' analysis: let E\mathcal{E}E be a topos over a topos of ZFC sets, Set\mathbf{Set}Set, and consider a subterminal object UUU distinct from 000. I claim that if N+U≅N\mathbb{N}+U \cong \mathbb{N}N+U≅N, then U≅1U \cong 1U≅1. This relies on countable extensivity, to the effect that the coproduct decomposition of N\mathbb{N}N pulls back along the isomorphism, and one of the pieces must be (part of) UUU, and hence this must be isomorphic to 111.
If that all works out, then since we always have N↪N+U↪N+1≅N\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N}+U \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N} + 1 \cong \mathbb{N}N↪N+U↪N+1≅N, we conclude that CH implies two-valuedness, and hence that E\mathcal{E}E is hyperconnected over Set\mathbf{Set}Set, so we can apply Jens' argument to deduce Booleanness.

Which form of CH are you using for the last implication?

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

Personally, I find this dissatisfying, and would prefer a formulation of CH that produces some more interesting cases. For example, we could demand that whenever N↪X↪ΩN\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow X \hookrightarrow \Omega^{\mathbb{N}}N↪X↪ΩN, there exists either a monomorphism X↪NX \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N}X↪N or ΩN↪X\Omega^{\mathbb{N}} \hookrightarrow XΩN↪X. This recovers the usual notion of cardinality in terms of injections in Set\mathbf{Set}Set, but avoids the cheap trick for localic toposes I describe above.

This feels like the right formulation. As you point out after, it's the 'constructive version' of SGL 'no epi' version.

Matteo Capucci (he/him) said:

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

... since we always have N↪N+U↪N+1≅N\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N}+U \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N} + 1 \cong \mathbb{N}N↪N+U↪N+1≅N, we conclude that CH implies two-valuedness...

Which form of CH are you using for the last implication?

I'm saying we can extend the sequence with N↪ΩN\mathbb{N} \hookrightarrow \Omega^{\mathbb{N}}N↪ΩN and argue that we don't have an isomorphism N+U≅ΩN\mathbb{N}+U \cong \Omega^{\mathbb{N}}N+U≅ΩN to conclude that we must have an isom with N\mathbb{N}N, to apply the preceding argument to.
So I guess the iso vs mono issue is secretly hiding some Cantor–Schröder–Bernstein issues. Since CSB only holds in Boolean toposes, one doesn't want to secretly force (hah) Booleanness in this way.
